sections.title: Regulations for critical information infrastructure
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary can issue regulations to implement this Act's provisions. These regulations may cover critical information infrastructure designations, protection measures, data transfer controls, storage protocols, audits, disaster recovery, electronic evidence procedures, and mutual legal assistance. The regulations must align with constitutional objectives and statutory instruments.

requirements.text: Cabinet Secretary may make regulations for better implementation of this Act
Regulations may provide for designation of computer systems as national critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for protection and preservation of critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for access, transfer and control of data in critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for storage and archiving of critical data
Regulations may provide for audit and inspection of national critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for recovery plans in case of disaster, breach or loss of critical infrastructure
Regulations may provide for standard operating procedures for electronic evidence collection
Regulations may provide for mutual legal assistance

sections.source_quote: 70. Regulations (1) The Cabinet Secretary may make regulations generally for the better carrying into effect of any provisions under this Act. (2) Without prejudice to the foregoing, regulations made under this section may provide for— (a) designation of computer systems, networks, programs, data as national critical information infrastructure; (b) protection, preservation and management of critical information infrastructure; (c) access to, transfer and control of data in any critical information infrastructure; (d) storage and archiving of critical data or information; (e) audit and inspection of national critical information infrastructure; (f) recovery plans in the event of disaster, breach or loss of national critical information infrastructure or any part of it; (g) standard operating procedures for the conduct, search, seizure and collection of electronic evidence; and (h) mutual legal assistance.

sections.heading: Short title
sections.title: Act's official name
sections.summary: This section defines the Act's official title as the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act. It establishes the legal citation for the legislation without imposing any obligations or conditions.

requirements.text: This Act may be cited as the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act.

sections.source_quote: This Act may be cited as the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Citation only, no compliance impact

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Key terms defined
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions for 30+ terms critical to the Act, including 'computer system', 'data', 'interception', 'critical infrastructure', and 'mobile money'. These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding computer-related offenses and cybercrimes in Kenya.

requirements.text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires—
"access" means gaining entry into or intent to gain entry by a person to a program or data stored in a computer system and the person either—
(a) alters, modifies or erases a program or data or any aspect related to the program or data in the computer system;
(b) copies, transfers or moves a program or data to—
(i) any computer system, device or storage medium other than that in which it is stored; or
(ii) to a different location in the same computer system, device or storage medium in which it is stored;
(c) causes it to be output from the computer in which it is held, whether by having it displayed or in any other manner; or
(d) uses it by causing the computer to execute a program or is itself a function of the program;
"Authority" means the Communications Authority of Kenya;

"authorised person" means an officer in a law enforcement agency or a cybersecurity expert designated by the Cabinet Secretary responsible for matters relating to national security by notice in the Gazette for the purposes of Part III of this Act;
"blockchain technology" means a digitized, decentralized, public ledger of all crypto currency transactions;
"Cabinet Secretary" means the Cabinet Secretary responsible for matters relating to internal security;
"Central Authority" means the Office of the Attorney-General and Department of Justice;
"Committee" means the National Computer and Cybercrimes Co-ordination Committee established under section 4;

"computer data storage medium" means a device, whether physical or virtual, containing or designed to contain, or enabling or designed to enable storage of data, whether available in a single or distributed form for use by a computer, and from which data is capable of being reproduced;

"computer system" means a physical or virtual device, or a set of associated physical or virtual devices, which use electronic, magnetic, optical or other technology, to perform logical, arithmetic storage and communication functions on data or which perform control functions on physical or virtual devices including mobile devices and reference to a computer system includes a reference to part of a computer system;
"content data" means the substance, its meaning or purport of a specified communication;
"critical information infrastructure system or data" means an information system, program or data that supports or performs a function with respect to a national critical information infrastructure;

"critical infrastructure" means the processes, systems, facilities, technologies, networks, assets and services essentials to the health, safety, security or economic well-being of Kenyans and the effective functioning of Government;
"cybersquatting" means the acquisition of a domain name over the internet in bad faith to profit, mislead, destroy reputation, or deprive another from registering the same, if the domain name is—
(a) similar, identical or confusingly similar to an existing trademark registered with the appropriate government agency at the time of registration;
(b) identical or in any way similar with the name of a person other than the registrant, in case of a personal name; or
(c) acquired without right or intellectual property interests in it;

"data" means any representation of facts, information or concepts in a form suitable for processing in a computer system, including a program suitable to cause a computer system to perform a function;

"interception" means the monitoring, modifying, viewing or recording of non-public transmissions of data to or from a computer system over a telecommunications system, and includes, in relation to a function of a computer system, listening to or recording a function of a computer system or acquiring the substance, its meaning or purport of such function;

"interference" means any impairment to the confidentiality, integrity or availability of a computer system, or any program or data on a computer system, or any act in relation to the computer system which impairs the operation of the computer system, program or data;
"mobile money" means electronic transfer of funds between banks or accounts' deposit or withdrawal of funds or payment of bills by mobile phone;
"national critical information infrastructure" means a vital virtual asset, facility, system, network or process whose incapacity, destruction or modification would have—

(a) a debilitating impact on the availability, integrity or delivery of essential services including those services, whose integrity, if compromised, could result in significant loss of life or casualties; or
(b) significant impact on national security, national defense, or the functioning of the state;
"network" means a collection of hardware components and computers interconnected by communications channels that allow sharing of resources and information;
"password" means any data by which a computer service or a computer system is capable of being obtained or used;

"pornography" includes the representation in books, magazines, photographs, films, and other media, telecommunication apparatus of scenes of sexual behaviour that are erotic or lewd and are designed to arouse sexual interest;

"premises" includes land, buildings, movable structures, a physical or virtual space in which data is maintained, managed, backed up remotely and made available to users over a network, vehicles, vessels or aircraft;

"program" means data representing instructions or statements that, if executed in a computer system, causes the computer system to perform a function and reference to a program includes a reference to a part of a program;
"requested State" means a state being requested to provide legal assistance under the terms of this Act;
"requesting State" means a state requesting for legal assistance and may for the purposes of this Act include an international entity to which Kenya is obligated;
"seize" with respect to a program or data includes to—
(a) secure a computer system or part of it or a device;
(b) make and retain a digital image or secure a copy of any program or data, including using an on-site equipment;
(c) render the computer system inaccessible;
(d) remove data in the accessed computer system; or
(e) obtain output of data from a computer system;
"service provider" means—
(a) a public or private entity that provides to users of its services the means to communicate by use of a computer system; and
(b) any other entity that processes or stores computer data on behalf of that entity or its users;

"subscriber information" means any information contained in the form of data or any form that is held by a service provider, relating to subscribers of its services, other than traffic data or content data, by which can be established—
(a) the type of communication service used, the technical provisions taken thereto and the period of service;

(b) the subscriber's identity, postal, geographic location, electronic mail address, telephone and other access number, billing and payment information, available on the basis of the service agreement or arrangement; or
(c) any other information on the site of the installation of telecommunication apparatus, available on the basis of the service agreement or arrangement;

"telecommunication apparatus" means an apparatus constructed or adapted for use in transmitting anything which is transmissible by a telecommunication system or in conveying anything which is transmitted through such a system;
"telecommunication system" means a system for the conveyance, through the use of electric, magnetic, electro-magnetic, electro-chemical or electro-mechanical energy, of—
(a) speech, music or other sounds;
(b) visual images;
(c) data;
(d) signals serving for the impartation, whether as between persons and persons, things and things or persons and things, of any matter otherwise than in the form of sound, visual images or data; or
(e) signals serving for the activation or control of machinery or apparatus and includes any cable for the distribution of anything falling within paragraphs (a), (b), (c) or (d);

"traffic data" means computer data relating to a communication by means of a computer system, generated by a computer system that formed a part in the chain of communication, indicating the communication's origin, destination, route, time, date, size, duration or the type of underlying service;
and
"trust accounts" means an account where a bank or trust company is holding funds in relation to mobile money on behalf of the public depositors.

sections.heading: Functions of the Committee
sections.title: Committee functions for cybersecurity and critical infrastructure
sections.summary: The Committee advises the Government on blockchain, critical infrastructure, mobile money, and trust accounts. It advises the National Security Council on cybercrimes, coordinates national security organs, receives cybercrime reports, develops critical information infrastructure frameworks, coordinates cyber threat analysis, cooperates with incident response teams, establishes cybersecurity standards, manages public key infrastructure, and trains on cybercrime prevention. The Committee may also perform other functions as granted by law or other statutes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the Committee's role in critical infrastructure security, which impacts data transfer assessments for systems designated as critical infrastructure due to their role in essential services, financial stability, and national security.

requirements.text: Advise the Government on security related aspects touching on blockchain technology, critical infrastructure, mobile money and trust accounts
Advise the National Security Council on computer and cybercrimes
Co-ordinate national security organs in matters relating to computer and cybercrimes
Receive and act on reports relating to computer and cybercrimes
Develop a framework to facilitate the availability, integrity and confidentiality of critical national information infrastructure
Co-ordinate collection and analysis of cyber threats, and response to cyber incidents that threaten cyberspace belonging to Kenya
Co-operate with computer incident response teams and other relevant bodies on response to threats of computer and cybercrime and incidents
Establish codes of cyber security practice and standards of performance for implementation by owners of critical national information infrastructure
Develop and manage a national public key infrastructure framework
Develop a framework for training on prevention, detection and mitigation of computer and cybercrimes
Perform any other function conferred on it by this Act or any other written law

sections.source_quote: The Committee shall— (a) advise the Government on security related aspects touching on matters relating to blockchain technology, critical infrastructure, mobile money and trust accounts; (b) advise the National Security Council on computer and cybercrimes; (c) co-ordinate national security organs in matters relating to computer and cybercrimes; (d) receive and act on reports relating to computer and cybercrimes; (e) develop a framework to facilitate the availability, integrity and confidentiality of critical national information infrastructure including telecommunications and information systems of Kenya; (f) co-ordinate collection and analysis of cyber threats, and response to cyber incidents that threaten cyberspace belonging to Kenya, whether such threats or incidents of computer and cybercrime occur within or outside Kenya; (g) co-operate with computer incident response teams and other relevant bodies, locally and internationally on response to threats of computer and cybercrime and incidents; (h) establish codes of cyber security practice and standards of performance for implementation by owners of critical national information infrastructure; (i) develop and manage a national public key infrastructure framework; (j) develop a framework for training on prevention, detection and mitigation of computer and cybercrimes and matters connected thereto; and (k) perform any other function conferred on it by this Act or any other written law.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: critical infrastructure coordination

sections.heading: Secretariat of the Committee
sections.title: Secretariat structure and responsibilities
sections.summary: The Secretariat consists of a Director and public officers approved by the Cabinet Secretaries. The Director is responsible for day-to-day administration, implementation of Committee decisions, staff management, financial record-keeping, budget preparation, and reporting to the Committee. The Director serves a four-year term without reappointment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the Secretariat's role in implementing critical infrastructure security frameworks, which affects data transfer assessments for systems requiring compliance with national cybersecurity standards.

requirements.text: There shall be a Secretariat which shall comprise of the Director and such number of public officers that, subject to the approval of the Committee, the Cabinet Secretary responsible for matters relating to internal security in consultation with the Cabinet Secretary responsible for matters relating to information, communications and technology may deploy to the Secretariat
The Director shall be the head of the Secretariat
The Director shall be responsible to the Committee for the day to day administration of the affairs of the Secretariat and implementation of the decisions arising from the Committee
The Director shall be responsible for the implementation of the decisions of the Committee
The Director shall be responsible for the efficient administration of the Secretariat
The Director shall be responsible for the management of staff of the Secretariat
The Director shall maintain accurate records on financial matters and resource use
The Director shall prepare and approve the budget for the required funding of the operational expenses of the Secretariat
The Director shall be appointed for a single term of four years and shall not be eligible for reappointment

sections.source_quote: There shall be a Secretariat which shall comprise of the Director and such number of public officers that, subject to the approval of the Committee, the Cabinet Secretary responsible for matters relating to internal security in consultation with the Cabinet Secretary responsible for matters relating to information, communications and technology may deploy to the Secretariat.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for data compliance

sections.heading: Critical information infrastructure
sections.title: Critical information infrastructure designation and protection
sections.summary: The Director designates systems as critical infrastructure if disruption would cause life-sustaining service interruptions, economic harm, massive casualties, money market failures, or security threats. The Director must inform owners of designation reasons and issue directives on data classification, storage, incident management, disaster recovery, security measures, compliance timelines, and other necessary cyber security actions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates critical infrastructure designation and security directives, directly impacting data transfer assessments for systems handling essential services, financial stability, and national security data.

requirements.text: The Director shall, by notice in the Gazette, designate certain systems as critical infrastructure
The Director shall designate a system as critical infrastructure if disruption would result in interruption of life sustaining services including water, health services and energy
The Director shall designate a system as critical infrastructure if disruption would result in adverse effect on the economy of the Republic
The Director shall designate a system as critical infrastructure if disruption would result in massive casualties or fatalities
The Director shall designate a system as critical infrastructure if disruption would result in failure or substantial disruption of the money market of the Republic
The Director shall designate a system as critical infrastructure if disruption would result in adverse and severe effect on the security of the Republic including intelligence and military services
The Director shall, within a reasonable time of designating a system as critical infrastructure, inform the owner or operator of the system the reasons for the designation
The Director shall issue directives to regulate classification of data held by critical information infrastructure
The Director shall issue directives to regulate protection, storing and archiving of data held by critical information infrastructure
The Director shall issue directives to regulate cyber security incident management by critical information infrastructure
The Director shall issue directives to regulate disaster contingency and recovery measures for critical information infrastructure
The Director shall issue directives to regulate minimum physical and technical security measures for critical information infrastructure
The Director shall specify the period within which the owner or person in control of critical information infrastructure must comply with directives
The Director shall issue directives on any other relevant matter necessary or expedient to promote cyber security in respect of critical information infrastructure

sections.source_quote: The Director shall, by notice in the Gazette, designate certain systems as critical infrastructure.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure protection directly impacts data security and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: Protection of critical information infrastructure
sections.title: Critical information infrastructure assessment and recommendations
sections.summary: The Committee must consult critical infrastructure owners to recommend entities for gazettment as critical information infrastructure. It then assesses cyber threats, economic harm from attacks, preparedness levels, and identifies security risks to protect public health and safety. These recommendations are made to critical infrastructure owners for securing systems against cyber threats.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires assessments of critical infrastructure vulnerabilities and economic impacts, which are relevant for DTIAs when transferring data across borders involving critical infrastructure systems.

requirements.text: The Committee shall submit recommendations of entities to be gazetted as critical information infrastructures within reasonable time after consulting with owners.
The Committee shall assess threats, vulnerabilities, risks, and cyberattack probabilities across critical infrastructure sectors.
The Committee shall determine economic harm from damage or unauthorized access to critical infrastructure.
The Committee shall measure preparedness of each sector against damage or unauthorized access.
The Committee shall identify risk-based security factors to protect public health, safety, or national socio-economic security.
The Committee shall recommend methods to secure critical infrastructure systems against cyber threats.

sections.source_quote: The Committee shall within reasonable time and in consultation with the owner or a person in control of an identified critical information infrastructure, submit to the National Security Council its recommendations of entities to be gazetted as critical information infrastructures.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure security assessments

sections.heading: Reports on critical information infrastructure
sections.title: Critical infrastructure incident reporting and response
sections.summary: Critical infrastructure owners must report cyber threats to the Committee, which then provides technical assistance to mitigate threats. The Director may independently investigate cybercrime attacks and secure critical infrastructure without entity reference. The Committee must periodically report on threats to the National Security Council.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates incident reporting and technical assistance for critical infrastructure, relevant for DTIAs when data transfers involve critical infrastructure systems.

requirements.text: Critical infrastructure owners must report incidents likely to constitute cyber threats to the Committee.
The National Security Council must provide technical assistance to mitigate threats after receiving reports.
The Director may investigate cybercrime attacks independently and secure critical infrastructure without entity reference.
The Director must submit periodic reports on cyber threats to the National Security Council.

sections.source_quote: The owner or operator of a system designated as critical infrastructure shall report to the Committee any incidents likely to constitute a threat in the nature of an attack that amounts to a computer and cybercrime and the action the owner or operator intends to take to prevent the threat.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification for critical infrastructure

sections.heading: Information sharing agreements
sections.title: Information sharing agreements for critical infrastructure
sections.summary: Private entities may share information with public entities on critical infrastructure for cyber security, crime investigations, life/property protection, or national security. Information sharing must exclude personal details identifying specific persons not directly related to cyber threats, and requires prior consent for health status information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs information sharing agreements for critical infrastructure, relevant for DTIAs when data transfers involve critical infrastructure systems.

requirements.text: Private entities may enter information sharing agreements with public entities on critical information infrastructure.
Agreements must only be for cyber security, crime investigations, life/property protection, or national security.
Parties must review information to remove personal details identifying specific persons not directly related to cyber threats.
No sharing of health status information without prior written consent of the person.

sections.source_quote: A private entity may enter into an information sharing agreement with a public entity on critical information infrastructure.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure data sharing with consent restrictions

sections.heading: Auditing of critical information infrastructures to ensure compliance
sections.title: Annual compliance reporting for critical information infrastructure audits
sections.summary: Critical information infrastructure owners must annually submit compliance reports to the Committee using a critical infrastructure framework. The Director must notify owners of audit dates and contact details before audits. The Director monitors audit effectiveness and may request additional information. Failure to comply with reporting, audits, or providing information constitutes an offence with fines up to KES 200,000 or imprisonment up to five years.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires annual compliance reporting for critical infrastructure audits, which impacts data transfer assessments by mandating structured reporting mechanisms for infrastructure security.

requirements.text: Critical information infrastructure owners must annually submit compliance reports to the Committee using a critical infrastructure framework
Director must notify owners of audit dates and contact details before audits
Director must monitor and report on audit adequacy and effectiveness
Director may request additional information from owners within a specified period
Failure to file compliance reports or cooperate with audits constitutes an offence
Failure to provide additional information within specified period constitutes an offence
Hindering audits or influencing committee members constitutes an offence
Failure to cooperate with auditors constitutes an offence
Failure to provide technical assistance to auditors constitutes an offence

sections.source_quote: The owner or person in control of a critical information infrastructure shall annually submit a compliance report on the critical information infrastructure to the Committee in line with a critical infrastructure framework in order to evaluate compliance.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure compliance reporting

sections.heading: Unauthorised access
sections.title: Unauthorised computer system access
sections.summary: Unauthorised access to computer systems is prohibited if the person lacks entitlement to control access or consent from entitled persons. Such access with intent to gain entry constitutes an offence with fines up to KES 5 million or imprisonment up to three years. The definition of unauthorised access is broad but immaterial whether the access targets specific programs, data, or systems.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines unauthorised access as a security breach that impacts data transfer assessments by establishing clear thresholds for when access constitutes a violation.

requirements.text: Access to a computer system is unauthorised if the person lacks entitlement to control access
Access to a computer system is unauthorised if the person lacks consent from entitled persons
Unauthorised access with intent to gain entry constitutes an offence
Fines up to KES 5 million or imprisonment up to three years for unauthorised access

sections.source_quote: A person who causes, whether temporarily or permanently, a computer system to perform a function, by infringing security measures, with intent to gain access, and knowing such access is unauthorised, commits an offence and is liable on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized access to systems with penalties

sections.heading: Access with intent to commit further offence
sections.title: Access with intent to commit further offences
sections.summary: Unauthorised access that is intended to facilitate further offences constitutes a more severe offence with fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to ten years. The definition of 'further offence' is broad but immaterial whether the intended offence occurs simultaneously with the access or later.
sections.dtia_summary: Increases penalties for data transfers involving malicious intent, relevant for DTIAs assessing risks of intentional data breaches.

requirements.text: Unauthorised access with intent to commit further offences constitutes an offence
Fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to ten years for such access

sections.source_quote: A person who commits an offence under section 14 with intent to commit a further offence under any law, or to facilitate the commission of a further offence by that person or any other person, commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybercrime offence with severe penalties for unauthorized access

sections.heading: Unauthorised interference
sections.title: Unauthorised interference with computer systems
sections.summary: Intentional unauthorised interference with computer systems is prohibited and carries fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to five years. More severe penalties apply if interference causes significant financial loss, threatens national security, causes physical injury, or threatens public health/safety. The definition of interference is broad but immaterial whether it targets specific systems or data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific safeguards for data transfers involving interference, relevant for DTIAs assessing risks of system disruptions.

requirements.text: Intentional unauthorised interference with computer systems constitutes an offence
Fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to five years for unauthorised interference
More severe penalties apply if interference causes significant financial loss
More severe penalties apply if interference threatens national security
More severe penalties apply if interference causes physical injury or death
More severe penalties apply if interference threatens public health or safety

sections.source_quote: A person who intentionally and without authorisation does any act which causes an unauthorised interference to a computer system, program or data, commits an offence and is liable on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybercrime penalties for unauthorized system interference

sections.heading: Unauthorised interception
sections.title: Unauthorised interception of data transmissions
sections.summary: Intentional unauthorised interception of data transmissions over telecommunication systems is prohibited and carries fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to five years. More severe penalties apply if interception causes significant financial loss, threatens national security, causes physical/psychological injury, or threatens public health/safety. The definition of interception is broad but immaterial whether it targets specific systems or data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific safeguards for data transfers involving interception, relevant for DTIAs assessing risks of data eavesdropping.

requirements.text: Intentional unauthorised interception of data transmissions constitutes an offence
Fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to five years for unauthorised interception
More severe penalties apply if interception causes significant financial loss
More severe penalties apply if interception threatens national security
More severe penalties apply if interception causes physical or psychological injury or death
More severe penalties apply if interception threatens public health or safety

sections.source_quote: A person who intentionally and without authorisation does any act which intercepts or causes to be intercepted, directly or indirectly and causes the transmission of data to or from a computer system over a telecommunication system commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding ten million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized interception of personal data transmissions

sections.heading: Illegal devices and access codes
sections.title: Illegal devices and access codes for cybercrime
sections.summary: Manufacturing, selling, or distributing devices, programs, passwords, or access codes designed for cybercrime is prohibited with fines up to KES 20 million or imprisonment up to ten years. Receiving or possessing such tools with intent to use them for cybercrime also constitutes an offence. Exceptions include authorised training, testing, or protection activities and compliance with judicial orders.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses tools used in data transfers, relevant for DTIAs assessing risks of malicious tools in data handling processes.

requirements.text: Manufacturing, selling, or distributing cybercrime tools constitutes an offence
Receiving or possessing cybercrime tools with intent to use them constitutes an offence
Fines up to KES 20 million or imprisonment up to ten years for manufacturing tools
Fines up to KES 10 million or imprisonment up to five years for receiving tools
Exceptions include authorised training, testing, or protection activities
Exceptions include compliance with judicial orders

sections.source_quote: A person who knowingly manufactures, adapts, sells, procures for use, imports, offers to supply, distributes or otherwise makes available a device, program, computer password, access code or similar data designed or adapted primarily for the purpose of committing any offence under this Part, commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding twenty million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits cybercrime tools, critical for data security compliance

sections.heading: Unauthorised disclosure of password or access code
sections.title: Unauthorised disclosure of computer access credentials
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they knowingly and without authority disclose a password, access code, or other means to gain access to a computer system. The offence carries a maximum fine of five million shillings or imprisonment for up to three years. If the disclosure causes wrongful gain, unlawful purpose, or loss, the penalty increases to a maximum fine of ten million shillings or imprisonment for up to five years.

requirements.text: A person who knowingly and without authority discloses any password, access code or other means of gaining access to any program or data held in any computer system commits an offence
A person liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or imprisonment for up to three years
For wrongful gain, unlawful purpose, or loss, liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding ten million shillings or imprisonment for up to five years

sections.source_quote: Unauthorised disclosure of password or access code (1) A person who knowingly and without authority discloses any password, access code or other means of gaining access to any program or data held in any computer system commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity breach of access credentials

sections.heading: Enhanced penalty for offences involving protected computer system
sections.title: Enhanced penalties for protected computer system offences
sections.summary: Offences involving protected computer systems carry enhanced penalties. A person who commits offences under sections 14, 15, 16, or 17 on a protected computer system faces a maximum fine of twenty-five million shillings or imprisonment for up to twenty years. Protected computer systems include critical infrastructure, national security systems, and essential public services.

requirements.text: Where a person commits any of the offences specified under sections 14, 15, 16 and 17 on a protected computer system, that person shall be liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding twenty five million shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding twenty years or both
Protected computer system means a computer system used directly in connection with, or necessary for, security, defence or international relations of Kenya
Protected computer system includes systems used for critical infrastructure, banking and financial services, payment systems, public utilities, and public transportation

sections.source_quote: Enhanced penalty for offences involving protected computer system (1) Where a person commits any of the offences specified under sections 14, 15, 16 and 17 on a protected computer system, that person shall be liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding twenty five million shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding twenty years or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Enhanced penalties for critical infrastructure breaches

sections.heading: Cyber espionage
sections.title: Cyber espionage against Kenya
sections.summary: Cyber espionage involves unlawfully accessing or intercepting critical data or national critical information infrastructure with the intent to benefit a foreign state against Kenya. Offenders face up to twenty years imprisonment or a fine of ten million shillings. Possession of data for such purposes also constitutes an offence with similar penalties.

requirements.text: A person who unlawfully and intentionally performs or authorizes another person to perform a prohibited act to gain access to critical data or national critical information infrastructure for the benefit of a foreign state against Kenya commits an offence
A person who unlawfully and intentionally possesses, communicates, delivers or makes available data to benefit a foreign state against Kenya commits an offence
A person who unlawfully and intentionally accesses or intercepts state-owned exempt information for the benefit of a foreign state against Kenya commits an offence

sections.source_quote: Cyber espionage (1) A person who unlawfully and intentionally performs or authorizes or allows another person to perform a prohibited act envisaged in this Act, in order to— (a) gain access, as provided under section 14, to critical data, a critical database or a national critical information infrastructure; or (b) intercept data, as provided under section 17, to, from or within a critical database or a national critical information infrastructure, with the intention to directly or indirectly benefit a foreign state against the Republic of Kenya, commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to imprisonment for a period not exceeding twenty years or to a fine not exceeding ten million shillings, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber espionage, critical data, foreign state, penalties

sections.heading: False publications
sections.title: False publications with malicious intent
sections.summary: Publishing false, misleading, or fictitious data with intent to be considered authentic constitutes an offence. Offenders face a maximum fine of five million shillings or imprisonment for up to two years. The offence is limited under constitutional freedom of expression when the false information propagates war, incites violence, constitutes hate speech, or negatively affects others' rights or reputations.

requirements.text: A person who intentionally publishes false, misleading or fictitious data or misinforms with intent that the data shall be considered or acted upon as authentic, with or without financial gain, commits an offence

The freedom of expression under Article 33 of the Constitution is limited for intentional publication of false data that propagates war, incites violence, constitutes hate speech, or negatively affects others' rights or reputations

sections.source_quote: False publications (1) A person who intentionally publishes false, misleading or fictitious data or misinforms with intent that the data shall be considered or acted upon as authentic, with or without any financial gain, commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybercrime offence with data falsification and penalties

sections.heading: Publication of false information
sections.title: Publication of false information causing public disorder
sections.summary: Publishing false information that causes panic, chaos, or violence among citizens or discredits the reputation of a person is an offence. Offenders face a maximum fine of five million shillings or imprisonment for up to ten years.

requirements.text: A person who knowingly publishes information that is false in print, broadcast, data or over a computer system, that is calculated or results in panic, chaos, or violence among citizens of the Republic, or which is likely to discredit the reputation of any person commits an offence

sections.source_quote: Publication of false information A person who knowingly publishes information that is false in print, broadcast, data or over a computer system, that is calculated or results in panic, chaos, or violence among citizens of the Republic, or which is likely to discredit the reputation of any person commits an offence and shall on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: False info causing public disorder relates to data misuse and cybercrime

sections.heading: Child pornography
sections.title: Child pornography offences
sections.summary: Publishing, producing, distributing, or possessing child pornography through computer systems is an offence. Offenders face a maximum fine of twenty million shillings or imprisonment for up to twenty-five years. Child pornography includes visual or audio material depicting children in sexually explicit conduct, with exceptions for works justified as public interest.

requirements.text: A person who intentionally publishes child pornography through a computer system commits an offence
A person who produces child pornography for publication through a computer system commits an offence

A person who downloads, distributes, transmits, disseminates, circulates, delivers, exhibits, lends for gain, exchanges, bars, sells or offers for sale child pornography through a computer system commits an offence
A person who possesses child pornography in a computer system or on a computer data storage medium commits an offence
It is a defence if the publication is justified as being for the public good in the interest of science, literature, learning or other objects of general concerns
Child means a person under eighteen years
Child pornography includes data depicting a child engaged in sexually explicit conduct
Child pornography includes data representing a child engaged in sexually explicit conduct

sections.source_quote: Child pornography (1) A person who intentionally— (a) publishes child pornography through a computer system; (b) produces child pornography for the purpose of its publication through a computer system; (c) downloads, distributes, transmits, disseminates, circulates, delivers, exhibits, lends for gain, exchanges, barter, sells or offers for sale, lets on hire or offers to let on hire, offers in another way, or make available in any way from a telecommunications apparatus pornography; or (d) possesses child pornography in a computer system or on a computer data storage medium, commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding twenty million or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding twenty five years, or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Child pornography offences directly impact data compliance via child data protection and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: Computer forgery
sections.title: Intentional computer data manipulation for legal purposes
sections.summary: This section defines computer forgery as intentionally altering, deleting, or suppressing computer data to create inauthentic data that is treated as genuine for legal purposes. The offender faces fines up to ten million shillings or imprisonment for up to five years. If the act is dishonest or for economic gain, the penalties increase to fines up to twenty million shillings or imprisonment for up to ten years.

requirements.text: A person who intentionally inputs, alters, deletes, or suppresses computer data resulting in inauthentic data that is considered or acted upon for legal purposes as if authentic commits an offence
Offender liable to fine not exceeding ten million shillings or imprisonment for up to five years or both
Dishonest or similar intent for wrongful gain, wrongful loss to another, or economic benefit results in fines up to twenty million shillings or imprisonment for up to ten years

sections.source_quote: 25. Computer forgery (1) A person who intentionally inputs, alters, deletes, or suppresses computer data, resulting in inauthentic data with the intent that it be considered or acted upon for legal purposes as if it were authentic, regardless of whether or not the data is directly readable and intelligible commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to fine not exceeding ten million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: defines computer forgery for data manipulation

sections.heading: Computer fraud
sections.title: Unlawful computer system access and data manipulation
sections.summary: This section defines computer fraud as gaining unlawful access to computer systems, modifying data, or causing harm through unauthorized actions. The offender faces fines up to twenty million shillings or imprisonment for up to ten years. It specifies that 'means' includes unauthorized access, data manipulation, system interference, data transfer, and other actions that facilitate fraud.

requirements.text: A person who unlawfully gains, occasions unlawful loss to another, or obtains economic benefit through means defined in subsection (2) commits an offence
For purposes of subsection (1), 'means' includes unauthorized access to computer systems, programs, or data
Means also includes input, alteration, modification, deletion, suppression, or generation of programs or data
Means includes interference, hindrance, impairment, or obstruction of computer system functioning
Means includes copying, transferring, or moving data to other systems or storage media
Means includes using data or program output from a computer system in any manner

sections.source_quote: 26. Computer fraud (1) A person who, with fraudulent or dishonest intent— (a) unlawfully gains; (b) occasions unlawful loss to another person; or (c) obtains an economic benefit for oneself or for another person, through any of the means described in subsection (2), commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding twenty million shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines computer fraud, data manipulation, and penalties

sections.heading: Cyber harassment
sections.title: Cyber harassment and protective orders
sections.summary: This section addresses cyber harassment by defining conduct that causes apprehension, fear of violence, property damage, or detrimental effects. Offenders face fines up to twenty million shillings or imprisonment for up to ten years. It allows courts to issue restraining orders, provide interim orders, and require service providers to share subscriber information for identification purposes.

requirements.text: A person who wilfully communicates with another person knowing or ought to know their conduct causes apprehension or fear of violence, property damage, or detrimental effects commits an offence
Offender liable to fine not exceeding twenty million shillings or imprisonment for up to ten years
Court may issue interim orders to compel refraining from communications
Court must hear and determine applications within fourteen days
Intermediaries may apply for orders on behalf of complainants
Court may order service providers to provide subscriber information for identification

sections.source_quote: 27. Cyber harassment (1) A person who, individually or with other persons, wilfully communicates, either directly or indirectly, with another person or anyone known to that person commits an offence, if they know or ought to know that their conduct— (a) is likely to cause those persons apprehension or fear of violence to them or damage or loss on that persons' property; or (b) detrimentally affects that person; or (c) is in whole or part, of an indecent or grossly offensive nature and affects the person.
sections.relevance_reason: Court orders for subscriber info for identification

sections.heading: Cybersquatting
sections.title: Unauthorised domain name registration
sections.summary: This section prohibits taking or using another person's name, business name, trademark, domain name, or other words/phrases without authority. Offenders face fines up to two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for up to two years.

requirements.text: A person who intentionally takes or makes use of a name, business name, trademark, domain name or other word or phrase registered, owned or in use by another person on the internet without authority commits an offence
Offender liable to fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for up to two years

sections.source_quote: 28. Cybersquatting A person who, intentionally takes or makes use of a name, business name, trademark, domain name or other word or phrase registered, owned or in use by another person on the internet or any other computer network, without authority or right, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized domain name use, relevant to data protection

sections.heading: Identity theft and impersonation
sections.title: Fraudulent use of electronic identifiers
sections.summary: This section prohibits fraudulently using another person's electronic signature, password, or unique identification feature. Offenders face fines up to two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for up to three years.

requirements.text: A person who fraudulently or dishonestly makes use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person commits an offence
Offender liable to fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for up to three years

sections.source_quote: 29. Identity theft and impersonation A person who fraudulently or dishonestly makes use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Fraudulent use of electronic identifiers directly impacts data security and privacy

sections.heading: Phishing
sections.title: Unlawful phishing for personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits creating or operating websites or sending messages to induce users to disclose personal information for unlawful purposes or gain unauthorized access to computer systems. Offenders face fines up to three hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for up to three years.

requirements.text: A person who creates or operates a website or sends a message with the intention to induce users to disclose personal information for unlawful purposes or gain unauthorized access to a computer system commits an offence
Offender liable to fine not exceeding three hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for up to three years

sections.source_quote: 30. Phishing A person who creates or operates a website or sends a message through a computer system with the intention to induce the user of a website or the recipient of the message to disclose personal information for an unlawful purpose or to gain unauthorized access to a computer system, commits an offence and is liable upon conviction to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits phishing for personal information

sections.heading: Interception of electronic messages or money transfers
sections.title: Unlawful destruction or abortion of electronic messages and money transfers
sections.summary: This section prohibits the unlawful destruction or abortion of electronic messages or money transfers. Offenders face fines up to 200,000 Kenyan shillings or imprisonment up to seven years. The offence applies to any person who intentionally interferes with electronic communications carrying financial or information content.

requirements.text: A person who unlawfully destroys or aborts any electronic mail or processes through which money or information is being conveyed commits an offence
Offenders are liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings
Offenders are liable on conviction to imprisonment not exceeding seven years

sections.source_quote: 31. Interception of electronic messages or money transfers A person who unlawfully destroys or aborts any electronic mail or processes through which money or information is being conveyed commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or to a term of imprisonment not exceeding seven years or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial crime and data destruction

sections.heading: Willful misdirection of electronic messages
sections.title: Willful misdirection of electronic messages
sections.summary: This section prohibits willful misdirection of electronic messages. Offenders face fines up to 100,000 Kenyan shillings or imprisonment up to two years. The offence applies to anyone who intentionally redirects electronic communications without authorization.

requirements.text: A person who willfully misdirects electronic messages commits an offence
Offenders are liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand shillings
Offenders are liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years

sections.source_quote: 32. Willful misdirection of electronic messages A person who willfully misdirects electronic messages commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized data redirection, critical for cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: Cyber terrorism
sections.title: Cyber terrorism involving computer systems
sections.summary: This section criminalizes accessing computer systems for terrorist purposes. Offenders face fines up to 5 million Kenyan shillings or imprisonment up to ten years. The term 'terrorist act' is defined under the Prevention of Terrorism Act (Cap. 59B).

requirements.text: A person who accesses or causes to be accessed a computer or computer system or network for purposes of carrying out a terrorist act commits an offence
Offenders are liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding five million shillings
Offenders are liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years
For the purpose of this section, 'terrorist act' shall have the same meaning as assigned under the Prevention of Terrorism Act (Cap. 59B)

sections.source_quote: 33. Cyber terrorism (1) A person who accesses or causes to be accessed a computer or computer system or network for purposes of carrying out a terrorist act, commits an offence and shall on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or to both. (2) For the purpose of this section, "terrorist act" shall have the same meaning as assigned under the Prevention of Terrorism Act (Cap. 59B).
sections.relevance_reason: Criminalizes cyber terrorism involving data systems

sections.heading: Inducement to deliver electronic message
sections.title: Inducing delivery of electronic messages
sections.summary: This section prohibits inducing someone to deliver electronic messages not intended for them. Offenders face fines up to 200,000 Kenyan shillings or imprisonment up to two years. The offence applies to anyone who deliberately causes electronic messages to be delivered to unintended recipients.

requirements.text: A person who induces any person in charge of electronic devices to deliver any electronic messages not specifically meant for him commits an offence
Offenders are liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings
Offenders are liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years

sections.source_quote: 34. Inducement to deliver electronic message A person who induces any person in charge of electronic devices to deliver any electronic messages not specifically meant for him commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized message delivery, cross-border data transfer risk

sections.heading: Intentionally withholding message delivered erroneously
sections.title: Intentionally withholding erroneously delivered messages
sections.summary: This section prohibits intentionally hiding or detaining electronic messages delivered in error. Offenders face fines up to 200,000 Kenyan shillings or imprisonment up to two years. The offence applies to anyone who knowingly retains messages intended for others but mistakenly received.

requirements.text: A person who intentionally hides or detains any electronic mail, message, electronic payment, credit and debit card which was found by the person or delivered to the person in error and which ought to be delivered to another person commits an offence
Offenders are liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings
Offenders are liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years

sections.source_quote: 35. Intentionally withholding message delivered erroneously A person who intentionally hides or detains any electronic mail, message, electronic payment, credit and debit card which was found by the person or delivered to the person in error and which ought to be delivered to another person, commits an offence and is liable on conviction a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits hiding erroneous messages, directly impacts data retention and breach prevention

sections.heading: Unlawful destruction of electronic messages
sections.title: Unlawful destruction of electronic messages
sections.summary: This section prohibits unlawful destruction or abortion of electronic messages or money transfers. Offenders face fines up to 200,000 Kenyan shillings or imprisonment up to two years. The offence applies to anyone who intentionally destroys electronic communications carrying financial or information content.

requirements.text: A person who unlawfully destroys or aborts any electronic mail or processes through which money or information is being conveyed commits an offence
Offenders are liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings
Offenders are liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years

sections.source_quote: 36. Unlawful destruction of electronic messages A person who unlawfully destroys or aborts any electronic mail or processes through which money or information is being conveyed commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unlawful destruction of financial data

sections.heading: Wrongful distribution of obscene or intimate images
sections.title: Wrongful distribution of intimate or obscene images
sections.summary: A person who transfers, publishes, or disseminates intimate or obscene images of another person commits an offence. They face a fine up to 200,000 shillings or imprisonment for up to two years, or both penalties.

requirements.text: A person who transfers, publishes, or disseminates intimate or obscene images of another person commits an offence

sections.source_quote: A person who transfers, publishes, or disseminates, including making a digital depiction available for distribution or downloading through a telecommunications network or though any other means of transferring data to a computer, the intimate or obscene image of another person commits an offence and is liable, on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Directly addresses prohibited data transfers of intimate images

sections.heading: Fraudulent use of electronic data
sections.title: Fraudulent use of electronic data
sections.summary: A person who knowingly alters, erases, inputs, or suppresses data stored in a computer without authority causes property loss and commits an offence. They face a fine up to 200,000 shillings or imprisonment for up to two years, or both. Similarly, sending materially misleading electronic messages that cause damage or loss, or manipulating electronic payments to short or overpay, also constitutes offences with similar penalties.

requirements.text: A person who knowingly and without authority causes any loss of property to another by altering, erasing, inputting or suppressing any data stored in a computer commits an offence
A person who sends an electronic message which materially misrepresents any fact upon which reliance by another person is caused to suffer any damage or loss commits an offence
A person who with intent to defraud, franks electronic messages, instructions, subscribes any electronic messages or instructions commits an offence
A person who manipulates a computer or other electronic payment device with the intent to short pay or overpay commits an offence

sections.source_quote: A person who knowingly and without authority causes any loss of property to another by altering, erasing, inputting or suppressing any data stored in a computer, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Fraudulent data manipulation and financial loss

sections.heading: Issuance of false e-instructions
sections.title: Issuance of false e-instructions
sections.summary: An employee authorized to use computers for financial transactions who issues false electronic instructions for debit, credit, or fund transfers commits an offence. They face a fine up to 200,000 shillings or imprisonment for up to two years, or both.

requirements.text: A person authorized to use a computer or other electronic devices for financial transactions including posting of debit and credit transactions, issuance of electronic instructions as they relate to sending of electronic debit and credit messages or confirmation of electronic fund transfer, issues false electronic instructions commits an offence

sections.source_quote: A person authorized to use a computer or other electronic devices for financial transactions including posting of debit and credit transactions, issuance of electronic instructions as they relate to sending of electronic debit and credit messages or confirmation of electronic fund transfer, issues false electronic instructions, commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial crime, false transactions, penalties

sections.heading: Reporting of cyber threat
sections.title: Reporting of cyber threats
sections.summary: Computer system operators must report cyber attacks, intrusions, or disruptions to the Committee within 24 hours. Reports must include breach details, affected individuals, risk assessment, and delays in informing affected persons. The Committee may isolate systems under suspicion of attacks. Non-compliance results in fines up to 200,000 shillings or imprisonment for up to two years.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely reporting of cyber incidents to regulators, which impacts data breach response and cross-border incident coordination.

requirements.text: A person who operates a computer system or a computer network shall immediately inform the Committee of any attacks, intrusions and other disruptions to the functioning of another computer system or network within twenty four hours of such attack, intrusion or disruption
A report made under subsection (1) shall include information about the breach, including a summary of how the breach occurred
A report made under subsection (1) shall include an estimate of the number of people affected by the breach
A report made under subsection (1) shall include an assessment of the risk of harm to the affected individuals
A report made under subsection (1) shall include an explanation of any circumstances that would delay or prevent the affected persons from being informed of the breach
The Committee may propose the isolation of any computer systems or network suspected to have been attacked or disrupted pending the resolution of the issues

sections.source_quote: (1) A person who operates a computer system or a computer network, whether public or private, shall immediately inform the Committee of any attacks, intrusions and other disruptions to the functioning of another computer system or network within twenty four hours of such attack, intrusion or disruption.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification and cyber threat reporting

sections.heading: Employee responsibility to relinquish access codes
sections.title: Employee responsibility to relinquish access codes
sections.summary: Employees must relinquish all access codes and rights to their employer's computer network immediately upon termination of employment, unless a contractual agreement specifies otherwise. Non-compliance results in fines up to 200,000 shillings or imprisonment for up to two years.

requirements.text: An employee shall, subject to any contractual agreement between the employer and the employee, relinquish all codes and access rights to their employer's computer network or system immediately upon termination of employment

sections.source_quote: An employee shall, subject to any contractual agreement between the employer and the employee, relinquish all codes and access rights to their employer's computer network or system immediately upon termination of employment.
sections.relevance_reason: Employee access rights post-employment

sections.heading: Aiding or abetting in the commission of an offence
sections.title: Aiding or abetting in commission of offences
sections.summary: A person who knowingly aids or abets any offence under this Act commits an offence. They face a fine up to 7 million shillings or imprisonment for up to four years, or both. Similarly, attempting to commit an offence or taking preparatory acts in furtherance of an offence also constitutes an offence with similar penalties.

requirements.text: A person who knowingly and willfully aids or abets the commission of any offence under this Act commits an offence
A person who knowingly and willfully attempts to commit an offence or does any act preparatory to or in furtherance of the commission of any offence under this Act commits an offence

sections.source_quote: A person who knowingly and willfully aids or abets the commission of any offence under this Act commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding seven million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding four years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party liability for cyber offences

sections.heading: Offences by a body corporate and limitation of liability
sections.title: Corporate liability for cyber offences and principal officer responsibilities
sections.summary: A body corporate is liable to a fine up to fifty million shillings if convicted of a cyber offence. Principal officers or those acting similarly are deemed to have committed the offence unless they prove they acted without consent/knowledge and exercised sufficient diligence to prevent it, facing fines up to five million shillings or imprisonment for up to three years.

requirements.text: Body corporate liable to fine not exceeding fifty million shillings on conviction for cyber offence
Principal officers or similar acting persons deemed to have committed offence unless they prove lack of consent/knowledge and sufficient diligence
Principal officers liable to fine up to five million shillings or imprisonment up to three years on conviction

sections.source_quote: Where any offence under this Act has been committed by a body corporate– (a) the body corporate is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding fifty million shillings; and (b) every person who at the time of the commission of the offence was a principal officer of the body corporate, or anyone acting in a similar capacity, is also deemed to have committed the offence, unless they prove that the offence was committed without their consent or knowledge and that they exercised such diligence to prevent the commission of the offence as they ought to have exercised having regard to the nature of their functions and to prevailing circumstances, and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Corporate liability for cyber offences and principal officer duties

sections.heading: Confiscation or forfeiture of assets
sections.title: Confiscation of assets derived from cyber offences
sections.summary: Courts can order confiscation or forfeiture of assets acquired using proceeds from cyber offences. Courts may also order restitution of assets gained from such offences in accordance with the Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act.

requirements.text: Court may order confiscation or forfeiture of monies, proceeds, properties and assets purchased or obtained by a person with proceeds derived from or in the commission of an offence
Court may order restitution of assets gained from commission of offence in accordance with Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act

sections.source_quote: A court may order the confiscation or forfeiture of monies, proceeds, properties and assets purchased or obtained by a person with proceeds derived from or in the commission of an offence under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Confiscation of cyber offence assets relates to financial data handling under AML/KYC.

sections.heading: Compensation order
sections.title: Compensation orders for cyber offence victims
sections.summary: Courts may order compensation payments to victims of cyber offences for resultant losses. Claims for damages are deemed satisfied to the extent of compensation paid, but civil remedies beyond compensation remain unaffected. Compensation orders are recoverable as civil debts.

requirements.text: Court may make order for payment of compensation to any person for resultant loss caused by cyber offence
Claims for damages are deemed satisfied to the extent of compensation paid
Compensation order is recoverable as a civil debt

sections.source_quote: Where the court convicts a person for any offence under this Part, or for an offence under any other law committed through the use of a computer system, the court may make an order for the payment by that person of a sum to be fixed by the court as compensation to any person for any resultant loss caused by the commission of the offence for which the sentence is passed.
sections.relevance_reason: Compensation orders affect financial claims but not direct data transfer rules

sections.heading: Additional penalty for other offences committed through use of a computer system
sections.title: Additional penalties for computer system offences
sections.summary: Offences committed through computer systems may incur penalties similar to other laws. Courts must consider the impact of computer use on the offence, commercial advantage, financial gain, breach of trust, number of victims, and other relevant factors when sentencing.

requirements.text: Person committing offence through computer system liable to penalty similar to that provided under other law
Court must consider manner in which computer use enhanced offence impact
Court must consider whether offence resulted in commercial advantage or financial gain
Court must consider value involved in consequential loss or damage or profit gained
Court must consider breach of trust or responsibility
Court must consider number of victims or persons affected by offence

sections.source_quote: A person who commits an offence under any other law through the use of a computer system commits an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a penalty similar to the penalty provided under that law.
sections.relevance_reason: Court sentencing factors for cyber offences

sections.heading: Scope of procedural provisions
sections.title: Applicability of procedural provisions to cyber offences
sections.summary: Procedural powers under this Act apply to cyber offences, other criminal offences using computer systems, and electronic evidence collection. Electronic evidence generated during investigations remains admissible regardless of its digital origin.

requirements.text: All powers and procedures under this Act applicable to cyber offences
All powers and procedures applicable to other criminal offences committed via computer systems
All powers and procedures applicable to collection of electronic evidence in criminal investigations
Electronic evidence generated during investigations remains admissible regardless of digital origin

sections.source_quote: All powers and procedures under this Act are applicable to and may be exercised with respect to any — (a) criminal offences provided under this Act; (b) other criminal offences committed by means of a computer system established under any other law; and (c) the collection of evidence in electronic form of a criminal offence under this Act or any other law.
sections.relevance_reason: Procedural powers for cyber offences and evidence admissibility

sections.heading: Search and seizure of stored computer data
sections.title: Search and seizure of computer data for investigations
sections.summary: Police may apply for warrants to search computer systems for evidence related to cyber offences. Warrants specify who may access data, where searches occur, and who may be searched. Obstructing, compromising integrity, or misusing these powers constitutes an offence.

requirements.text: Police or authorized person may apply for warrant to enter premises to access, search and seize computer data for criminal investigation
Warrant must identify police officer or authorized person
Warrant must direct police to seize data in question
Warrant may direct police to search persons identified in warrant
Warrant may direct police to enter and search premises identified in warrant
Warrant may direct police to search persons found on or at premises
Warrant valid until executed or cancelled by issuing court
Police must present warrant copy to person against whom it is issued
Obstructing lawful exercise of powers constitutes an offence
Compromising integrity or confidentiality of computer system constitutes an offence
Misusing powers granted under this section constitutes an offence

sections.source_quote: Where a police officer or an authorised person has reasonable grounds to believe that there may be in a specified computer system or part of it, computer data storage medium, program, data that— (a) is reasonably required for the purpose of a criminal investigation or criminal proceedings which may be material as evidence; or (b) has been acquired by a person as a result of the commission of an offence, the police officer or the authorised person may apply to the court for issue of a warrant to enter any premises to access, search and similarly seize such data.
sections.relevance_reason: Warrant requirements for data access

sections.heading: Record of and access to seized data
sections.title: Seized data access and record-keeping for law enforcement
sections.summary: After a search or seizure under section 48, the searcher must create a list of seized data with seizure date and provide it to the premises occupier or system controller. Law enforcement may permit access to seized data for specified individuals under certain conditions, but can refuse access if it risks criminal activity or undermines investigations. Courts may override refusal to allow access for qualified individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires law enforcement to maintain records of seized data and permits access under strict conditions, relevant for assessing data access during investigations involving cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Make a list of seized data with seizure date and provide to premises occupier or system controller
Law enforcement may permit access to seized data for individuals with custody or control of the system
Law enforcement may refuse access if it risks criminal activity or undermines investigations
