Court may allow access for qualified individuals despite refusal by law enforcement

sections.source_quote: Where a computer system or data has been removed or rendered inaccessible, following a search or a seizure under section 48, the person who made the search shall, at the time of the search or as soon as practicable after the search—(a) make a list of what has been seized or rendered inaccessible, and shall specify the date and time of seizure; and (b) provide a copy of the list to the occupier of the premises or the person in control of the computer system referred to under paragraph (a).
sections.relevance_reason: Law enforcement data access restrictions

sections.heading: Production order
sections.title: Court-ordered data production for investigations
sections.summary: Law enforcement may request court orders to compel specified persons or service providers to submit computer data or subscriber information necessary for investigations. The court directs the submission of data held by the person or service provider within their control, ensuring data is relevant to the investigation.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for law enforcement to obtain data from service providers for investigations, relevant for assessing data access and transfer during cross-border investigations.

requirements.text: Law enforcement may apply to court for order to compel data submission from persons or service providers
Court directs specified person to submit computer data in their possession or control
Court directs service provider to submit subscriber information in their possession or control

sections.source_quote: Where a police officer or an authorised person has reasonable grounds to believe that specified data stored in a computer system or a computer data storage medium is in the possession or control of a person in its territory; and specified subscriber information relating to services offered by a service provider in Kenya are in that service provider's possession or control and is necessary or desirable for the purposes of the investigation, the police officer or the authorised person may apply to court for an order.
sections.relevance_reason: Court-ordered data production for investigations

sections.heading: Expedited preservation and partial disclosure of traffic data
sections.title: Expedited traffic data preservation for investigations
sections.summary: Law enforcement must issue notices requiring expeditious preservation of traffic data or partial disclosure to identify service providers when data is at risk of modification or loss. Preserved data must be maintained for up to thirty days, with possible extensions if the court deems preservation necessary for investigations without excessive cost.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires immediate preservation of traffic data during investigations, relevant for assessing data retention and transfer risks in cross-border communications.

requirements.text: Law enforcement must issue notice requiring expeditious preservation of traffic data or partial disclosure to identify service providers
Preserved data must be maintained for up to thirty days
Court may extend preservation period if necessary for investigations without excessive cost

sections.source_quote: Where a police officer or an authorised person has reasonable grounds to believe that any specified traffic data stored in any computer system or computer data storage medium or by means of a computer system is reasonably required for the purposes of a criminal investigation; and there is a risk or vulnerability that the traffic data may be modified, lost, destroyed or rendered inaccessible, the police officer or an authorised person shall serve a notice on the person who is in possession or control of the computer system, requiring the person to—(i) undertake expeditious preservation of such available traffic data regardless of whether one or more service providers were involved in the transmission of that communication; or (ii) disclose sufficient traffic data concerning any communication in order to identify the service providers and the path through which communication was transmitted.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer and breach notification

sections.heading: Real-time collection of traffic data
sections.title: Real-time traffic data collection for investigations
sections.summary: Law enforcement may request court orders to collect traffic data in real-time from service providers for specific investigations. The court requires detailed explanations on data types, users, offenses, and privacy measures before issuing orders. Real-time collection is limited to six months, with possible extensions if necessary for investigations without excessive cost.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows real-time data collection for investigations, relevant for assessing data transfer timing and privacy during cross-border investigations.

requirements.text: Law enforcement may apply to court for real-time traffic data collection order
Court requires detailed explanations on data types, users, offenses, and privacy measures
Real-time collection limited to six months
Court may extend order if necessary for investigations without excessive cost

sections.source_quote: Where a police officer or an authorised person has reasonable grounds to believe that traffic data associated with specified communications and related to the person under investigation is required for the purposes of a specific criminal investigation, the police officer or authorised person may apply to the court for an order to—(a) permit the police officer or authorised person to collect or record through the application of technical means traffic data, in real-time; (b) compel a service provider, within its existing technical capability—(i) to collect or record through application of technical means traffic data in real time; or (ii) to cooperate and assist a police officer or an authorised person in the collection or recording of traffic data, in real-time, associated with specified communications in its jurisdiction transmitted by means of a computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Court orders for real-time data collection with privacy safeguards

sections.heading: Interception of content data
sections.title: Real-time content data interception for investigations
sections.summary: Law enforcement may request court orders to intercept content data in real-time for specific investigations. The court requires detailed explanations on content data types, offenses, and privacy measures before issuing orders. Real-time collection is limited to nine months, with possible extensions if necessary for investigations without excessive cost.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows real-time content data interception for investigations, relevant for assessing data transfer timing and privacy during cross-border communications.

requirements.text: Law enforcement may apply to court for real-time content data interception order
Court requires detailed explanations on content data types, offenses, and privacy measures
Real-time collection limited to nine months
Court may extend order if necessary for investigations without excessive cost

sections.source_quote: Where a police officer or an authorised person has reasonable grounds to believe that the content of any specifically identified electronic communications is required for the purposes of a specific investigation in respect of an offence, the police officer or authorised person may apply to the court for an order to—(a) permit the police officer or authorised person to collect or record through the application of technical means; (b) compel a service provider, within its existing technical capability—(i) to collect or record through the application of technical means; or (ii) to co-operate and assist the competent authorities in the collection or recording of, content data, in real-time, of specified communications within the jurisdiction transmitted by means of a computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: real-time data interception for investigations

sections.heading: Obstruction and misuse of power
sections.title: Penalties for obstruction and misuse of law enforcement powers
sections.summary: Persons obstructing lawful exercise of powers or failing to comply with requirements face fines up to five million shillings or imprisonment for three years. Police officers misusing powers also face similar penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets penalties for obstruction and misuse of law enforcement powers, relevant for assessing compliance with data transfer regulations.

requirements.text: Obstruction of lawful exercise of powers or failure to comply with requirements is punishable by fine up to five million shillings or imprisonment for three years
Misuse of powers by police officers is punishable by fine up to five million shillings or imprisonment for three years

sections.source_quote: A person who obstructs the lawful exercise of the powers under this Part, including destruction of data, or fails to comply with the requirements of this Part is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for obstruction not directly related to data compliance

sections.heading: Appeal
sections.title: Appeal rights for court decisions
sections.summary: Any individual affected by a court decision under this Part can appeal to the High Court or Court of Appeal within thirty days of the decision. The appeal must be filed within this timeframe to be valid. This section establishes the procedural mechanism for challenging court rulings related to cybercrime matters.

requirements.text: Any person aggrieved by a court decision may appeal to the High Court or Court of Appeal within thirty days

sections.source_quote: Any person aggrieved by any decision or order of the Court made under this Part, may appeal to the High Court or Court of Appeal as the case may be within thirty days from the date of the decision or order.
sections.relevance_reason: Appeal procedure unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: Confidentiality and limitation of liability
sections.title: Service provider liability exemptions
sections.summary: Service providers are exempt from civil or criminal liability if they lack actual notice, knowledge, or willful intent regarding contraventions. They also cannot be held liable for service provision or data disclosures made in compliance with this Act or legal powers. This section defines when service providers are protected from liability.

requirements.text: Service provider not liable unless established to have actual notice, knowledge, or willful intent
Service provider not liable for maintaining and making available their service
Service provider not liable for data disclosures made only as required by this Act or compliance with powers

sections.source_quote: A service provider shall not be subject to any civil or criminal liability, unless it is established that the service provider had actual notice, actual knowledge, or willful and malicious intent, and not merely through omission or failure to act, had thereby facilitated, aided or abetted the use by any person of any computer system controlled or managed by a service provider in connection with a contravention of this Act or any other written law.
sections.relevance_reason: Service provider liability exemptions

sections.heading: General principles relating to international cooperation
sections.title: International legal cooperation principles
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for mutual legal assistance between Kenya and foreign states in cybercrime investigations. It specifies that requests for cooperation can be made for investigations, evidence collection, or traffic data preservation. The Central Authority may grant or refuse requests while ensuring confidentiality and specific usage conditions for the data shared.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes international data transfer mechanisms for law enforcement cooperation, requiring that data shared for investigations be used solely for the specified criminal matter and kept confidential.

requirements.text: Mutual legal assistance requests apply in addition to Mutual Legal Assistance Act and Extradition Act
Requests for mutual legal assistance may be made for investigations, evidence collection, or traffic data preservation
Central Authority may grant or refuse legal assistance requests
Requested State must keep information confidential
Requested State must use information only for the specified criminal matter

sections.source_quote: The Central Authority may make a request for mutual legal assistance in any criminal matter to a requested State for purposes of— (a) undertaking investigations or proceedings concerning offences related to computer systems, electronic communications or data; (b) collecting evidence of an offence in electronic form; or (c) obtaining expeditious preservation and disclosure of traffic data, real-time collection of traffic data associated with specified communications or interception of content data or any other means, power, function or provisions under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Mutual legal assistance procedures for data sharing

sections.heading: Spontaneous information
sections.title: Spontaneous information sharing with foreign states
sections.summary: The Central Authority can share information with foreign states without prior request if it believes the disclosure will assist in criminal investigations or lead to cooperation requests. The information may be kept confidential or subject to specific conditions, and the foreign state must notify the Central Authority if it cannot comply with these conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for spontaneous sharing of information across borders for law enforcement purposes, with conditions to ensure confidentiality and specific use of data.

requirements.text: Central Authority may forward information to foreign states without prior request
Central Authority may request information be kept confidential or subject to conditions
Foreign state must notify Central Authority if unable to comply with specified conditions

sections.source_quote: The Central Authority may, subject to this Act and other relevant law, without prior request, forward to a foreign State information obtained within the framework of its own investigations when it considers that the disclosure of such information might assist the foreign State in initiating or carrying out investigations or proceedings concerning criminal offences or might lead to a request for co-operation by the foreign State under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data sharing with foreign states

sections.heading: Expedited preservation of stored computer data
sections.title: Expedited preservation of computer data
sections.summary: A foreign state may request the Central Authority to preserve computer data within Kenya for criminal investigations. The request must specify the authority, offence details, data to preserve, custodian information, necessity, and intent to seek further assistance. Preserved data remains for at least 120 days to allow the foreign state to submit additional requests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables expedited data preservation across borders for law enforcement, with strict requirements for data preservation duration and purpose.

requirements.text: Requesting State may request expedited preservation of computer data within Kenya
Request must specify authority, offence, data to preserve, custodian information, necessity, and intent for further assistance
Preserved data remains for at least 120 days

sections.source_quote: Subject to section 57, a requesting State which has the intention to make a request for mutual legal assistance for the search or similar access, seizure or similar securing or the disclosure of data, may request the Central Authority to obtain the expeditious preservation of data stored by means of a computer system, located within the territory of Kenya.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer for criminal investigations

sections.heading: Expedited disclosure of preserved traffic data
sections.title: Expedited disclosure of traffic data
sections.summary: When a foreign state is involved in a communication, the Central Authority may quickly disclose traffic data to identify the service provider and transmission path. This facilitates cross-border investigations by providing necessary technical information without delay.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables rapid disclosure of traffic data across borders to identify service providers involved in communications, supporting international law enforcement cooperation.

requirements.text: Central Authority discloses traffic data to identify service provider and transmission path

sections.source_quote: Where during the course of executing a request under section 57 with respect to a specified communication, the investigating agency discovers that a service provider in another State was involved in the transmission of the communication, the Central Authority shall expeditiously disclose to the requesting State a sufficient amount of traffic data to identify that service provider and the path through which the communication was transmitted.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer investigation

sections.heading: Mutual assistance regarding accessing of stored computer data
sections.title: Cross-border access to computer data via mutual assistance
sections.summary: A requesting State can request Kenya's Central Authority to search, seize, or disclose computer data stored within Kenya, including data preserved under section 60. The request must include specific details about the criminal matter, purpose, relevant facts, and requested actions. The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including warrants, before executing the request. The Central Authority may then seek cooperation from the requesting State during the search and seizure.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific information to be provided by a requesting State for cross-border data access, including criminal context and data details. The Central Authority must obtain authorisation before executing the request, creating a framework for international data sharing with safeguards.

requirements.text: Requesting State must provide name of authority conducting investigation
Requesting State must describe nature of criminal matter and relevant facts
Requesting State must state purpose of request and type of assistance sought
Requesting State must provide details of offence, investigation, and relevant orders for asset restraint
Requesting State must specify procedures for the requested State to follow
Requesting State must include confidentiality wishes and reasons
Requesting State must state period within which request must be complied with
Requesting State must provide details of property, computer, or electronic device to be traced
Requesting State must specify stored computer data and its relationship to the offence
Requesting State must provide custodian details or location of computer system
Requesting State must include agreement on payment of damages or costs
Requesting State must provide any other information assisting request execution
Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation including warrants
Central Authority may seek support and cooperation from requesting State
Central Authority must provide search and seizure results to requesting State

sections.source_quote: Mutual assistance regarding accessing of stored computer data (1) Subject to section 57, a requesting State may request the Central Authority to search or similarly access, seize or similarly secure, and disclose data stored by means of a computer system located within the territory of Kenya, including data that has been preserved in accordance with section 60.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data access with legal safeguards

sections.heading: Trans-border access to stored computer data with consent or where publicly available
sections.title: Cross-border access to publicly available computer data
sections.summary: Police officers or authorized persons may access publicly available computer data located anywhere globally without restriction. They may also access computer data in another territory if the person with lawful authority to disclose the data provides lawful and voluntary consent through a computer system in Kenya.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows cross-border access to publicly available data without restrictions, but requires consent for data in other territories. This section is relevant for DTIAs involving public data transfers.

requirements.text: Police officer or authorized person may access publicly available stored computer data regardless of geographic location
Police officer or authorized person may access computer data in another territory if person with lawful authority provides lawful and voluntary consent

sections.source_quote: A police officer or authorised person may, subject to any applicable provisions of this Act—(a) access publicly available stored computer data, regardless of where the data is located geographically; or (b) access or receive, through a computer system in Kenya, stored computer data located in another territory, if such police officer or authorised person obtains the lawful and voluntary consent of the person who has the lawful authority to disclose the data through that computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border access to personal data by police

sections.heading: Mutual assistance in the real-time collection of traffic data
sections.title: Real-time cross-border traffic data collection
sections.summary: A requesting State may request Kenya's Central Authority to provide real-time traffic data collection assistance for specified communications in Kenya. The request must include details about the authority, offence, data location, purpose, and identification of traffic data. The Central Authority must obtain authorisation before executing the request and may seek cooperation from the requesting State.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific information for real-time traffic data collection across borders, with the Central Authority needing authorisation before execution. This section is relevant for DTIAs involving real-time data transfers.

requirements.text: Requesting State must specify authority seeking use of powers
Requesting State must specify offence and brief summary of related facts
Requesting State must name authority with access to relevant traffic data
Requesting State must specify location where traffic data may be held
Requesting State must state intended purpose for required traffic data
Requesting State must provide sufficient information to identify traffic data
Requesting State must provide any further details relevant to traffic data
Requesting State must specify necessity for use of powers
Requesting State must state terms for use and disclosure of traffic data to third parties
Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation including warrants
Central Authority may seek support and cooperation from requesting State
Central Authority must provide results of measures to requesting State

sections.source_quote: Mutual assistance in the real-time collection of traffic data (1) Subject to section 57, a requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide assistance in real-time collection of traffic data associated with specified communications in Kenya transmitted by means of a computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer authority

sections.heading: Mutual assistance regarding the interception of content data
sections.title: Real-time cross-border content data interception
sections.summary: A requesting State may request Kenya's Central Authority to provide real-time collection or recording of content data for specified communications in Kenya. The request must include details about the authority, offence, communication, purpose, and data identification. The Central Authority must obtain authorisation before executing the request and may seek cooperation from the requesting State.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific information for real-time content data interception across borders, with the Central Authority needing authorisation before execution. This section is relevant for DTIAs involving real-time data transfers.

requirements.text: Requesting State must specify authority seeking use of powers
Requesting State must specify offence and brief summary of related facts
Requesting State must name authority with access to relevant communication
Requesting State must specify location or nature of communication
Requesting State must state intended purpose for required communication
Requesting State must provide sufficient information to identify communications
Requesting State must provide details of relevant interception data
Requesting State must specify recipient of communication
Requesting State must state intended duration for use of communication
Requesting State must specify necessity for use of powers
Requesting State must state terms for use and disclosure of communication to third parties
Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation including warrants
Central Authority may seek support and cooperation from requesting State
Central Authority must provide results of measures to requesting State

sections.source_quote: Mutual assistance regarding the interception of content data (1) Subject to section 57, a requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide assistance in the real-time collection or recording of content data of specified communications in the territory of Kenya transmitted by means of a computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data interception powers

sections.heading: Point of contact
sections.title: 24/7 point of contact for cybercrime investigations
sections.summary: The Central Authority must designate a 24/7 point of contact for cybercrime investigations to provide technical advice, preserve data, and collect evidence. The point of contact must be resourced to securely communicate with international counterparts and coordinate access to mutual assistance under this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a dedicated international point of contact for cybercrime investigations, enabling real-time coordination of cross-border data assistance. This is relevant for DTIAs involving international cooperation.

requirements.text: Central Authority must designate 24/7 point of contact for cybercrime investigations
Point of contact must provide technical advice
Point of contact must preserve data pursuant to sections 59 and 60
Point of contact must collect evidence and locate suspects within expeditious timelines
Point of contact must be resourced with capacity to securely communicate internationally
Point of contact must coordinate and enable access to international mutual assistance

sections.source_quote: The Central Authority shall ensure that the investigation agency responsible for investigating cybercrime, shall designate a point of contact available on a twenty-four hour, seven-day-a-week basis, in order to ensure the provision of immediate assistance for the purpose of investigations or proceedings concerning criminal offences related to computer systems and data, or for the collection of evidence in electronic form of a criminal offence, including carrying out the following measures—(a) the provision of technical advice; (b) the preservation of data pursuant to sections 59 and 60; (c) the collection of evidence, the provision of legal information, and locating of suspects, within expeditious timelines to be defined by regulations under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border cybercrime coordination

sections.heading: Territorial jurisdiction
sections.title: Territorial jurisdiction for cybercrime offences
sections.summary: Offences under this Act are tried in Kenyan courts if committed in Kenya. Acts or omissions outside Kenya that would constitute offences in Kenya are deemed committed in Kenya if the perpetrator is a Kenyan citizen, ordinarily resident in Kenya, or the act targets Kenyan citizens, government property, or compels Kenyan government action, or the perpetrator is present in Kenya.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when cybercrime offences are subject to Kenyan jurisdiction, relevant for data transfers involving Kenyan citizens or government property. This section impacts data residency and jurisdictional considerations.

requirements.text: Offences under this Act are tried in Kenyan courts if committed in Kenya
Acts or omissions outside Kenya deemed committed in Kenya if perpetrator is Kenyan citizen
Acts or omissions outside Kenya deemed committed in Kenya if perpetrator is ordinarily resident in Kenya
Acts or omissions outside Kenya deemed committed in Kenya if act targets Kenyan citizens
Acts or omissions outside Kenya deemed committed in Kenya if act targets government property outside Kenya
Acts or omissions outside Kenya deemed committed in Kenya if act compels Kenyan government action
Acts or omissions outside Kenya deemed committed in Kenya if perpetrator is present in Kenya

sections.source_quote: Territorial jurisdiction (1) Any court of competent jurisdiction shall try any offence under this Act where the act or omission constituting the offence is committed in Kenya. (2) For the purposes of subsection (1), an act or omission committed outside Kenya which would if committed in Kenya constitute an offence under this Act is deemed to have been committed in Kenya if—(a) the person committing the act or omission is–(i) a citizen of Kenya; or (ii) ordinarily resident in Kenya; and (b) the act or omission is committed–(i) against a citizen of Kenya; (ii) against property belonging to the Government of Kenya outside Kenya; or (iii) to compel the Government of Kenya to do or refrain from doing any act; or (c) the person who commits the act or omission is, after its commission or omission, present in Kenya.
sections.relevance_reason: Territorial jurisdiction for cybercrime

sections.heading: Forfeiture
sections.title: Forfeiture of apparatus used in cybercrime
sections.summary: Courts can order the forfeiture of apparatus, devices, or items used in cybercrime offenses to the designated authority as an additional penalty. This applies to individuals convicted under this Act. The forfeiture covers items directly related to the offense or its commission.

requirements.text: The court may order forfeiture of any apparatus, device or thing used in connection with the commission of cybercrime offenses

sections.source_quote: 67. Forfeiture The court before which a person is convicted of any offence may, in addition to any other penalty imposed, order the forfeiture of any apparatus, device or thing to the Authority which is the subject matter of the offence or is used in connection with the commission of the offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: forfeiture not directly related to data compliance

sections.heading: Prevailing Clause
sections.title: Cybercrime laws override other laws
sections.summary: This section states that cybercrime provisions in this Act take precedence over conflicting provisions in other laws. It ensures that the Act's rules are the primary authority for cybercrime matters, even if other laws address similar issues.

requirements.text: Provisions of this Act supersede any other law regarding cybercrimes in case of conflict

sections.source_quote: 68. Prevailing Clause Whenever there is a conflict between this Act and any other law regarding cybercrimes, the provisions of this Act shall supersede any such other law.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybercrime laws override other laws

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for critical information infrastructure
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary can issue regulations to implement this Act's provisions. These regulations may cover critical information infrastructure designations, protection measures, data transfer controls, storage protocols, audits, disaster recovery, electronic evidence procedures, and mutual legal assistance. The regulations must align with constitutional objectives and statutory instruments.

requirements.text: Cabinet Secretary may make regulations for better implementation of this Act
Regulations may provide for designation of computer systems as national critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for protection and preservation of critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for access, transfer and control of data in critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for storage and archiving of critical data
Regulations may provide for audit and inspection of national critical information infrastructure
Regulations may provide for recovery plans in case of disaster, breach or loss of critical infrastructure
Regulations may provide for standard operating procedures for electronic evidence collection
Regulations may provide for mutual legal assistance

sections.source_quote: 70. Regulations (1) The Cabinet Secretary may make regulations generally for the better carrying into effect of any provisions under this Act. (2) Without prejudice to the foregoing, regulations made under this section may provide for— (a) designation of computer systems, networks, programs, data as national critical information infrastructure; (b) protection, preservation and management of critical information infrastructure; (c) access to, transfer and control of data in any critical information infrastructure; (d) storage and archiving of critical data or information; (e) audit and inspection of national critical information infrastructure; (f) recovery plans in the event of disaster, breach or loss of national critical information infrastructure or any part of it; (g) standard operating procedures for the conduct, search, seizure and collection of electronic evidence; and (h) mutual legal assistance.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure data transfer and security
